revision.text:
'''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Artifacts in Tokyo National Museum]] [[Category:Inscriptions on swords]] [[Category:pp]] [[Category:Tōdaijiyama Kofun]] [[Category:pp]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kkkk]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Artifacts in Tokyo National Museum]] [[Category:Inscriptions on swords]] [[Category:pp]] [[Category:Tōdaijiyama Kofun]] [[Category:pp]] [[Category:Default sort converted to citation needed]] {{citation needed|Tōdaijiyama Kofun}}[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:deprecated qq open]]

The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area aqqroximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, aqqroximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area aqqroximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, aqqroximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Burial Mound|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (burial mound) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The burial mound is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient burial mounds are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area aqqroximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, aqqroximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Kofun|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The Kofun is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient Kofuns are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area a11roximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, a11roximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Kofun|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The Kofun is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient Kofuns are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area [[Category:pp error]]approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, [[Category:pp error]]approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Kofun|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Kofun|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The Kofun is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. "Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The Kofun is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient Kofuns are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area [[Category:pp error]]approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, [[Category:pp error]]approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Kofun|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] '''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. revision.text:
'''Tōdaijiyama Kofun''' (東大寺山古墳) is a {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} (Kofun) located in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|ja|天理市}}, Nara Prefecture. The Kofun is of the {{ill|zenpō-kōen-fun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped mound) type, and its grave goods have been designated as a {{ill|National Treasure of Japan|ja|国宝}}. It is believed to have been constructed in the mid-4th century during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. Among its grave goods is a sword with an engraved inscription featuring 24 characters in gold inlay, including a date referencing the "Zhongping" (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty, corresponding to the years 184–189 CE. == Overview == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is situated in a hilly area spanning Wani to Ichinomoto in Tenri City. This region was the base of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏}}, and many related ancient Kofuns are found along the ridges, about 70 meters above the surrounding plain. The kofun is one of several attributed to this influential clan. On the same ridge lies the largest {{ill|late Yayoi period|ja|弥生時代}} {{ill|highland settlement|ja|高地性集落}} in Nara Prefecture. The settlement covers an area approximately 400 meters east to west and 300 meters north to south. It featured pit dwellings surrounded by a double moat, with a structure similar to that of the {{ill|Kannonyama Site|ja|観音寺山遺跡}} in Izumi, Osaka Prefecture. The Tōdaijiyama Kofun overlaps this earlier settlement, and it is estimated to have been constructed 150–200 years after the settlement ceased to function. == Dimensions, Shape, and Burial Facilities == The Tōdaijiyama Kofun is a zenpō-kōen-fun, approximately 140 meters in length. The mound is surrounded by a ring of {{ill|cylindrical haniwa|ja|円筒埴輪|1=Cylindrical Haniwa}}. Its construction is estimated to date to the late 4th century. The burial facility within the posterior circular portion consists of a {{ill|clay coffin|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|1=Clay coffin chamber}} covering a wooden coffin aligned parallel to the mound's main axis. The burial chamber, measuring an estimated 9.4 meters in length, was a large-scale structure but had been looted. == Grave Goods == The burial chamber contained two burial pits to the east and west, which housed an abundance of weapons and other grave goods. Among these, a sword with an inscription referencing the Zhongping (中平) era of the Later Han Dynasty was discovered in the eastern burial pit. The grave goods include 20 iron swords, 9 iron daggers, various types of jade, stone armlets, and steatite jars. Five swords from the burial site feature decorative pommels, including a "Zhongping" inscribed sword. The sword guards exhibit various designs, predominantly trefoil-shaped rings similar to those found at {{ill|Sekiganri Kofun|ja|石巌里古墳}} in Lelang. Such swords were also excavated from the {{ill|Waka Hachiman Shrine Kofun|ja|若八幡宮古墳}} in Fukuoka. One of the swords features a pommel shaped like a house, resembling depictions of buildings on mirrors, such as the {{ill|House-patterned Mirror|ja|家屋文鏡}} from the {{ill|Samida Takarazuka Kofun|ja|佐味田宝塚古墳}} in Kawai, Nara Prefecture. === The "Zhongping" Inscribed Sword === A 110-centimeter-long iron sword featuring a 24-character gold-inlay inscription was excavated. The inscription reads:
"Zhongping □□ (year), fifth month, day of Bingwu: This sword, forged of refined steel, was created and inscribed. It is a blade of great purity and strength, aligned with the stars above and averting misfortune below."
The inscription references the Zhongping era of Emperor Ling of Han, corresponding to 184–189 CE. This period marks the end of the "Wa Kingdom's Turmoil" (倭国乱) mentioned in Chinese records. The sword is believed to have been a gift from the Later Han dynasty following the end of the turmoil. While its exact provenance and how it came to be buried in the Tōdaijiyama Kofun remain uncertain, the sword suggests a link between the local elites and the Later Han dynasty. == Cultural Properties == === National Treasure === The grave goods from the Tōdaijiyama Kofun were collectively designated as a National Treasure of Japan in 2017. They are currently housed in the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|ja|東京国立博物館}}. The designation includes the following items: - 1 gold-inlaid flower-decorated ring-pommel sword - 2 flower-decorated ring-pommel swords - 2 house-shaped ring-pommel swords - 249 metal objects - 102 stone objects - 59 jade pieces - 2 organic material fragments - (Accompanying designation) 9 fragments of bronze arrowheads - (Accompanying designation) Miscellaneous stone fragments - (Accompanying designation) 4 fragments of haniwa == References == == Further Reading == - Supervising Organization: {{ill|Independent Administrative Institution for Cultural Properties|ja|文化財研究所}}, Nara Division, "Japanese Archaeology: The 'Dawn of Civilization' Exhibition," Shogakukan, 2005, ISBN 4-09-681821-6. == External Links == - [http://www.tnm.jp/modules/r_free_page/index.php?id=1394 Tokyo National Museum: Special Exhibition on Tōdaijiyama Kofun Swords (2011)] {{citation needed|Tōdaijiyama Kofun}} {{citation needed|Tōdaijiyama Kofun}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{Kofun navbar}} {{draft categories| [[Category:pp error]] [[Category:Kofun in Japan]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture's history]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Artifacts in Tokyo National Museum]] [[Category:Inscriptions on swords]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tōdaijiyama Kofun]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:New]] }} [[sn:Tōdaijiyama Kofun]] [[en:Tōdaijiyama Kofun]] [[ja:東大寺山古墳]] [[sn:ōdaijiyama Kofun]]
